Glossary About
Islamic Tradition

al-Fārābī

c. 872 – 950

The “Second Master”: a systematic philosophy of intellect that frames the Islamic reception of Aristotle.

A faithful report on al-Farabi’s philosophy of mind cannot isolate “mind” from cosmology, metaphysics, logic, politics, and religion. In his corpus, what a modern reader would call philosophy of mind is distributed across his doctrine of the soul, his noetics of intellect, his theory of imagination, his account of language and thought, and his hierarchy of divine and separate intellects. That means that discussions of God, spiritual beings, and angels are not detachable ornamentation. They belong to the architecture by which al-Farabi explains how human cognition is possible and what its highest perfection is.

The most defensible overall reconstruction is this: al-Farabi offers a graded theory of mind in which human cognition begins in embodied sensation, rises through imagination and rational abstraction, depends for its full actualization on the Active Intellect, and culminates in a state of intellectual perfection ordered to felicity. At the same time, language, religion, law, and politics matter because most human beings do not grasp truth demonstratively; they grasp it imaginatively and symbolically. So his philosophy of mind is not only a psychology of individual cognition but also a theory of how truth is represented, communicated, and socially inhabited.

1. The upper reaches of mind: God, separate intellects, celestial minds

If one speaks of the “mind of God” in al-Farabi, one must do so analogically and carefully. The First is not a psyche alongside other psyches. It is pure actuality, utterly immaterial, and therefore intellect, intelligible, and act of intellection in one undivided essence. Its knowing is not a discursive process and is not something added to its essence. In the strictest philosophical presentation, al-Farabi’s First directly knows itself; the question whether all other things are known in that self-knowledge arises only derivatively through its causal status. He is also careful to say that emanation does not mean that God performs an extra act or undergoes a motion in order to create. Rather, the First’s very existence is causally sufficient for the existence of what depends on it.

Below the First stand the separate intellects. In The Perfect State, the Second thinks itself and thinks the First; from this ordered procession of immaterial intellects come the successive heavens and, ultimately, the Active Intellect. These are genuine intellective beings, not decorative cosmological names. Their cognition is self-reflexive and hierarchical: they know themselves and the First, and their position in the order of being determines their rank. In the philosophical register, these are separate intellects; in al-Farabi’s religious register, the same order is restated as God, spiritual beings, and angels.

The Active Intellect is the last of the separate intellects and the one most directly relevant to human mind. It governs the sublunary world, supplies first principles for human understanding, makes the potential intellect actual, and makes potential intelligibles actual. Al-Farabi also insists that it is not the First Principle itself: it is always at its own perfection, but its action depends on finding suitably prepared subjects and matter. This is important for distinguishing divine cognition from angelic or intermediary cognition. The First is radically self-sufficient; the Active Intellect is perfect but mediating.

Al-Farabi even attributes a kind of intellective life to the heavens. The celestial bodies are not merely mechanical structures. Their forms are actual intellects; they think the separate intellect from which they derive their being and think the First. Yet because they also have a substratum, they are not pure intellect through and through. In that respect, al-Farabi says, they differ from the First and the separate intellects and share something with human beings. This gives him a genuinely graduated ontology of minds: divine intellect, separate intellects or angelic intellects, celestial intellective beings, and human rational souls.

2. Human soul: embodied origin, immaterial telos

At the human level, al-Farabi begins from an Aristotelian hylomorphism. Soul is the actuality or form of the living body, not a ghost accidentally lodged in matter. Matter is the substratum of form, and form does not subsist as that particular bodily actuality without matter; yet matter itself is what it is only through form. His psychology is therefore not originally dualistic. It is biological and developmental. He even embeds it in a physiology in which the heart is the primary ruling organ and the brain a secondary ruling organ subordinate to it.

But this hylomorphism is not flat or terminal. Al-Farabi also holds that the rational part can perfect itself so far that it no longer needs the body as matter for its own subsistence. In that sense the human soul is embodied in origin but ordered toward a degree of separation. During ordinary embodied life the intellect needs sensation and imagination; at a higher stage it may no longer need body for existence, and at the highest stage it no longer needs bodily instruments even for its acts. The best label for this is a graded or developmental hylomorphism with an immaterial telos.

3. Faculty psychology: what “mind” includes

A comprehensive Farabian philosophy of mind must include more than intellect alone. In the broad sense, soul or mind is a hierarchy of powers: nutritive power, the five external senses, appetite, imagination or representation, and rational faculty. These arise in order, with each higher level presupposing and governing lower ones. Human beings share nutrition and sensation with lower living things, share appetite and imagination with animals, and are distinguished by the rational faculty through which they attain intelligibles, sciences, arts, and moral discrimination.

His account is also organizational. The senses do not float free; they report into a ruling sensory arrangement, a kind of common sense, located in the heart in his physiology. Imagination, too, is located in the heart and has no distributed auxiliary organs. It retains sensible forms after the objects are absent, compares them, separates and recombines them, and produces representations that may be either true or false. The rational faculty then rules over imagination, sensation, and nutrition. This is a sharply hierarchical but deeply integrated model.

Al-Farabi’s theory of action is equally important. Will is not a sovereign faculty independent of cognition. It is an inclination in the appetitive faculty toward or away from what has been apprehended by sensation, representation, or reason. That means that action always has both a cognitive and a desiderative component. One does not simply “know” and then separately “will”; desire is awakened by the way an object is mentally presented. This is one reason imagination matters so much: it can move the appetites by presenting objects under affectively charged images.

Within reason itself, al-Farabi distinguishes practical and theoretical rationality. Practical reason concerns what can be done, altered, calculated, or deliberated about; theoretical reason concerns what is necessary and cannot be otherwise. Practical reason is ultimately subordinate to theoretical reason, and theoretical reason is not for the sake of anything further beyond felicity. In modern terms, his philosophy of mind is openly teleological and normative: cognition is ordered to the good life, not merely to neutral representation.

4. Intellect in the narrow sense: al-‘aql

Al-Farabi’s treatise On the Intellect is central because it shows how wide the semantic field of ‘aql really is. He distinguishes six senses: ordinary discernment, the theologians’ appeal to what intellect accepts or rejects, the faculty that knows the first principles of theoretical science, the practical prudence discussed in Aristotle’s Ethics, the fourfold intellect of Aristotle’s De Anima, and the separate intellect of Metaphysics. This already tells us something important: “intellect” in al-Farabi ranges from everyday practical wisdom all the way to divine or separate noetic actuality.

In the specifically psychological sense, the potential or material intellect is a faculty or part of the soul prepared to abstract forms from matter and receive them as its own forms. Al-Farabi uses the famous wax analogy: the intellect is like matter for the forms it receives, yet unlike bodily matter it receives them through and through. Once forms are actualized in it, the intellect does not remain a bare container standing apart from its contents. It becomes those forms. That is why actual intellect, actual intellection, and actual intelligible converge.

The acquired intellect is the stage at which intellect can know immaterial forms without needing to abstract them from matter. In al-Farabi’s own ordering, the acquired intellect stands between human actual intellect and the Active Intellect; it is like form in relation to the lower intellect and like matter in relation to the higher. The Active Intellect, by contrast, is a separate form never in matter, and its relation to the potential intellect is like the relation of the sun to sight: it gives the principle by which the potential knower becomes actual knower and the potentially intelligible becomes actually intelligible.

This gives rise to one of the major interpretive questions: is knowledge for al-Farabi abstraction from sensibles, or emanation or illumination from the Agent/Active Intellect? The most careful answer is that it is both. He explicitly says the potential intellect abstracts forms from their material conditions; he also explicitly says that the Active Intellect makes intellect and intelligibles actual and even places forms in matter. Modern scholarship has therefore treated the issue as a real debate. The most faithful reconstruction is a hybrid model: abstraction is the human cognitive process, while illumination by the Active Intellect is the metaphysical condition of its success and completion.

The same hybrid structure explains his account of first principles. In one register, intellect is the non-discursive faculty by which the soul grasps the necessary, universal, first premises of science without syllogistic derivation. In another register, the Active Intellect provides the first principles through which human beings can attain happiness. These are not contradictory accounts. The first describes how first principles are possessed in the soul; the second explains their cosmic source and enabling cause.

5. Concepts, language, internal speech, and truth

Al-Farabi’s logic and philosophy of language are indispensable to his philosophy of mind because they explain how mental content is structured. He treats primary concepts as arising from perception of the external world. Words are then conventionally attached to those concepts, and language ideally develops so as to mirror the ordering of concepts. Concepts can combine into compound concepts, and language imitates this as well. This is, in effect, a doctrine of compositional thought.

He also says that demonstrations and syllogisms fundamentally occur in internal speech, not external speech. External language is especially important for pedagogy because most students cannot directly inspect the ordering of concepts in the mind and need words as a bridge. This implies a fairly sophisticated picture of thought as structured, inwardly articulate, and not reducible to spoken language. It also means that linguistic disorder can damage cognition by obscuring conceptual relations.

His theory of truth reinforces the point. A concept is “true” when what is in the soul is the same in the external world. Truth for him is therefore not restricted to full propositions; even non-propositional concepts can be assessed in terms of correspondence or failure of correspondence. Teaching proceeds through conceptualization, assent or verification, and memory. So al-Farabi’s philosophy of mind includes a theory of mental content, truth conditions, and the pedagogical transition from language to thought.

6. Imagination: the pivot of human mentality

Imagination is one of the great keys to al-Farabi’s system. It is not a minor faculty of fancy. It retains sensible impressions when the external object is gone, recombines them, and mediates between sense and reason. In waking life it works alongside sensation, appetite, and reason; in sleep, when those other faculties are quiet, it manipulates its stored forms more freely. It is therefore both mnemonic and productive.

More strikingly, imagination can imitate not only sensible things but also intelligibles, desires, bodily temperaments, and emotions. Through such imitation it can stir bodily organs and move action. That is why al-Farabi can explain dream action, emotional arousal, and symbolic cognition within one faculty. Imagination is the place where cognition becomes image, affect, and psychosomatic movement. In a modern vocabulary, it is the crucial bridge among representation, emotion, and embodiment.

This same faculty underwrites prophecy. Because imagination is closely tied to rationality, the Active Intellect can sometimes act on it directly, providing either particulars in sensible form or sensible imitations of intelligibles. These may appear especially in dreams but, in rare and perfected cases, also in waking vision. Present and future particulars can thus be received without discursive deliberation, while higher intelligibles are received under imagistic likenesses. Al-Farabi describes this as the highest perfection of imagination.

In the supreme human case, revelation descends from God through the Active Intellect to the perfected human rational and representative faculties. Al-Farabi explicitly says that when this happens in both theoretical and practical reason and in representation, the person becomes at once wise, philosopher, and visionary prophet. This is not anti-rational ecstasy. It is the crowning integration of perfected intellect and perfected imagination.

7. Religion, angels, and symbolic cognition

This is the point at which comments about God and angels become indispensable for philosophy of mind rather than external theology. Al-Farabi says that religion consists of opinions and actions prescribed by the first ruler for the sake of ultimate happiness. He also says that religion stands to philosophy as a likeness or symbolic representation: what philosophy presents scientifically, religion presents through metaphor, affinity, and image. In religious works, the same order described philosophically as the First and the separate intellects appears as God, spiritual beings, and angels.

That is why the angelic register should be taken seriously. In al-Farabi’s own architecture, the minds of angels or spiritual beings are not irrelevant to the human mind; they belong to the same noetic order that grounds revelation, prophecy, law, and civic education. The philosopher knows this order demonstratively. Most citizens know it through symbols impressed on imagination. So the transition from metaphysics to religion is not a departure from philosophy of mind. It is al-Farabi’s explanation of how minds with different capacities can participate in one truth.

8. Ethics, politics, and the formation of mind

Al-Farabi’s philosophy of mind is ethical to the core. Repeated actions form dispositions of soul. Good actions strengthen and perfect the soul until it becomes capable of felicity; bad actions deform it. He even compares the process to acquiring an art through practice. Mind is not a neutral spectator here. Habit writes itself into the soul’s very form.

This also explains cognitive corruption. Al-Farabi says that diseased souls can come to enjoy what is bad, just as bodily illness can distort taste. Habit and will can spoil the faculty of representation itself, so that the soul either recoils from the good or cannot present it properly at all. Cognitive error, then, is not just false judgment. It can be moral-psychological deformation. This is one reason his ethics and psychology are inseparable.

The political dimension follows immediately. Philosophers know the highest truths by strict demonstration and insight. Others know them by trust, assent, and symbolic imitation. Demonstration yields certainty; persuasion yields assent. A healthy political order is therefore one that educates souls at their proper level without cutting them off from reality. In al-Farabi, the polity is partly an epistemic institution and partly a therapeutic institution for minds.

9. Death, survival, and personal identity

In the extant works, the perfected soul can dispense with matter and survive bodily death. Once separate from matter, bodily predicates no longer apply to it, and al-Farabi describes posthumous grades of felicity corresponding to the grades of perfection achieved in life. He also says that souls remain differentiated because their dispositions were conditioned by different bodily temperaments and histories. So posthumous plurality is real, not merely illusory.

At the same time, survival is not universal in the same way for all. Al-Farabi says that the souls of the ignorant remain imperfect and need matter for their preservation; when the bodily substrate perishes, they perish and ultimately dissolve. By contrast, some wicked souls survive in misery because right views had partly released them from matter while vicious dispositions turned that survival into perpetual distress. So his afterlife psychology is stratified: felicity, misery, or dissolution depending on the soul’s intellectual and moral formation.

Here one must add a serious caution. Reports by Ibn Bajja, Ibn Tufayl, and Averroes about al-Farabi’s lost commentary on Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics say that there he denied immortality and conjunction with the Active Intellect, whereas the extant works affirm both. Because the commentary is lost, the tension cannot be fully resolved. Any rigorous reconstruction has to acknowledge that the extant Farabian philosophy of mind is strong on immortality and conjunction, but the broader textual tradition leaves a real interpretive problem.

Personal identity therefore remains partly unresolved. On the one hand, the texts preserve multiplicity of surviving souls and differentiated felicities. On the other hand, al-Farabi describes the highest state as the soul being united “as it were” with the Active Intellect and becoming as close as possible to it. That points toward a transpersonal ideal of perfected intellect. The safest conclusion is that embodied history individuates souls, while the highest intellectual perfection pushes them toward a mode of being whose center of gravity is no longer private psychological individuality in the ordinary sense.

10. The comprehensive philosophy of mind we can extract

The most compact extraction is this.
1. Mind is hierarchical actuality, not a single homogeneous substance. Human mentality includes nutrition, sensation, appetite, imagination, and reason; above human reason stand the Active Intellect, the separate intellects, and finally the First.
2. Human mind is embodied in origin but not necessarily in destiny. The soul begins as the form of a body and ordinarily depends on bodily faculties, yet its rational perfection tends toward a state that can subsist without body.
3. Higher cognition does not bypass imagination; it uses and perfects it. Imagination is the mediator between sensible form and intelligible content, between cognition and desire, and between philosophy and religion.
4. Thought is representational, compositional, and internally articulated. Primary concepts arise from perception, language conventionally signifies them, demonstrations occur in internal speech, and truth consists in correspondence between what is in the soul and what is in the world.
5. Knowing is both abstraction and illumination. Human intellect abstracts forms from sensibles, but the Active Intellect is the external, immaterial condition that actualizes intellect and intelligibles and supplies the first principles of understanding.
6. Mind is intrinsically normative and practical. Cognition is ordered to felicity; practical reason, appetite, and habituation shape what the soul can present to itself as good or bad.
7. Mind is intrinsically political and pedagogical. Most people do not know by demonstration but by persuasion and symbol, so civic religion and law are part of the social economy of cognition.
8. Divine and angelic intellects are integral to the theory, not marginal additions. The First, the separate intellects, and the Active Intellect define the upper horizon of what intellect is and explain how human thought, revelation, and prophecy are possible.
9. The system is coherent but textually unstable at one major point: immortality and conjunction. The extant works strongly support survival and union-like perfection; the testimony about the lost ethical commentary prevents complete dogmatism about al-Farabi’s final position.

Conclusion

Taken as a whole, al-Farabi’s philosophy of mind is neither merely Aristotelian faculty psychology nor merely Neoplatonic cosmology. It is a unified theory in which embodied cognition, abstraction, imagination, language, symbolic representation, prophecy, political pedagogy, and posthumous intellectual destiny all belong to one graduated order of mind. The shortest accurate formula would be: al-Farabi offers a graded hylomorphism under a cosmology of intellect, with imagination as the central mediator and felicity as the final end. In that framework, discourse about God and angelic or separate intellects is not a distraction from philosophy of mind. It is part of the very map by which he explains what mind is, how it knows, and what it can become.
